beats.text: Here's matrix A — a two-by-two with entries a, b in row one and c, d in row two.
The inverse formula: one over the determinant of A, times the matrix d, negative b, negative c, a. But why those entries? Each one is a cofactor.
Strike row one and column one — the entry left standing is d. That's the one-one cofactor. It lands in position one-one of the inverse.
Now the entry negative b. Strike row two and column one. The survivor is b — but position two plus one is odd, so the sign flips. Cofactor: negative b.
Same logic fills every slot. The full matrix of cofactors, transposed, gives exactly d, negative b, negative c, a.
Divide by the determinant — that's Cramer's rule built right into the inverse formula.
